front: Play: characters like Hothead and Glasses wait at the title location; Glasses checks his watch and realizes ten years have passed
Play: the set consists of two iron railings arranged in the form of a cross; a single sign is the only other set piece
Play: characters include the Old Man, Girl, Mother, Carpenter, and the Silent Man, who discuss their reasons for traveling into the city
Play: Hothead wants to eat trendy yogurt in this absurdist play
Play: a character practices English with phrases like 'Open your pigs!'
Play: the Old Man claims he is going to play chess against Li Mosheng
Play: the cast chants 'Go!' before finally leaving the title location
Play: deemed 'Spiritual Pollution' upon release; only performed 13 times in its original run
Play: includes 'polyphonic dialogue' where characters speak simultaneously; a leitmotif represents the Silent Man
Play: this Chinese absurdist play shares its English title with a William Inge play set in rural Kansas
Play: in its original run, every production was preceded by a performance of Lu Xun's The Passerby
Play: a plastic sheet brought by the Carpenter lets characters take shelter from rain
Novel: characters called 'I' and 'You' (and sometimes 'She' and 'He') travel through rural China; chapters alternate between their perspectives
Novel: inspired by the author's false lung cancer diagnosis; the narrator flees Beijing after the misdiagnosis
Novel: set in rural Sichuan province; the narrator travels into the countryside
Novel: the narrator meets a frog incarnation of God whose unblinking eye appears in the novel's final chapter
Novel: the narrator hears about sasquatch-like 'Wild Men' while traveling on a rickety bus
Novel: the narrator listens to folk songs from ethnic minorities like the Miao and Yi
Novel: 'You' searches for the mythical title location, Lingshan
Novel: an ancient tablet says 'history is a lie'; the narrator makes faux translations like 'history is prediction' and 'history clangs like iron'
Novel: the narrator hears a legend about a nun who cut open her stomach when bathing
Novel: the narrator's grandmother died in an old people's home after being sent for thought reform
Novel: an 'optional' chapter where a critic accuses the author of being a nihilist of the 'searching-for-roots' school
Novel: an old man states, 'The road is not wrong, it is the traveller who is wrong'
Novel: translated into English by Mabel Lee; described as a mix of fiction, travelogue, and autobiography
Novel: exemplifies a style focusing on the individual voice that its author termed 'cold literature'
Novel: a Taoist monk explains that the character's 'tragedy' is returning to 'a normal life among normal people'
Novel: a tale concerns an elderly carpenter who dies upon finishing a carving of a malevolent goddess
Play: opens with twelve actors playing a 'rope game' demonstrating complex relationships; the Lead Actor guides characters across a river
Play: a Woman emerges from Oblivion and teaches the Crowd how to use language, but the Crowd turns against her and kills her
Play: the Card Player rigs a game; losers must stick slips of paper to their faces
Play: the Man discovers that the Card Player has rigged the game, but the Crowd tries to convince the Man he is wrong
Play: features a thirteenth-generation Dogskin Plaster Seller who appears after the Woman is murdered
Play: can be performed at 'the hall of a temple, a circus tent, or any empty space'
Play: opening stage directions say it occurs on 'the nonexistent other shore'
Play: the protagonist finds a key after crawling under the Stable Keeper, then hears the voice of the Shadow
Play: the title location is mentioned in the Heart Sutra; a character asks about lights there and is told 'there are flowers, lots of flowers'
Novel: set in Hong Kong; the semi-autobiographical protagonist argues with Margarethe, a Jewish woman, in a hotel room
Novel: juxtaposes sexual encounters with scenes from the Cultural Revolution
Work: the narrator buys a fiberglass fishing rod after seeing it in a store window; stores it on his toilet tank
back: Buying a Fishing Rod for My Grandfather (Gao Xingjian)
work: Buying a Fishing Rod for My Grandfather

front: Work: the narrator searches for a stone bridge near a filled-in lake but learns all bridges were replaced by asphalt roads

front: Play: a Tramp and a Thug meet the titular person after he is accused of murdering a prostitute
Author: wrote plays in French including Between Life and Death and Weekend Quartet
Author: wrote Absolute Signal, featuring a character called Trumpet who carries a trumpet as an assistant train conductor
Author: created a 'dance-drama' based on Li Qingzhao's poem 'Shengshengman,' blending music, poetry, and dance
Author: a play by this author ends with actors returning to stage commenting on mundane matters while sounds like bicycle bells, running water, and ambulance sirens play
Author: self-described 'nothingist' who lives 'Without isms' (from a 1996 essay)
Author: advocates 'cold literature'; claimed 'literature has no duty to the masses'
Author: notable translator of Samuel Beckett's works into Chinese
Author: creates minimalistic ink landscape paintings that provide the covers of many of his own books
Author: this playwright's works were banned in China for references to the Tiananmen Square massacre and for being critical of the Communist Party
Author: first encounter with Western art was a 1980 trip; worked as a French interpreter for Ba Jin, the author of The Family, whose protagonists share this author's surname
Author: wrote an essay on 'National Character in Fiction' comparing 'linked chapter' and 'direct portrayal' forms of prose
Author: Mabel Lee is this author's frequent English translator
